answer:
The argument has premises \( Q \equiv R \) and \( \sim(S \lor Q) \), with conclusion \( R \).  
For the premises to be true, \( Q \) and \( R \) must have the same truth value, and \( S \lor Q \) must be false, meaning both \( S \) and \( Q \) are false.  
If \( Q \) is false, then \( R \) must also be false, making the conclusion false, so the argument is invalid with a counterexample when \( Q \), \( S \), and \( R \) are all false.  

\boxed{A}
